chapters.name: Quadratic Equations
errors: Wrong factorisation or sign mistake in splitting the middle term.
Using the quadratic formula with incorrect b or discriminant.
Not writing both roots clearly.
secrets: Prefer factorisation when easy; otherwise use formula neatly.
Write D = b^2 - 4ac first, then substitute.
Box both roots separately.

chapters.name: Arithmetic Progressions
errors: Using wrong n (term number) or wrong formula for nth term/sum.
Mixing up a (first term) and d (common difference).
Arithmetic mistakes in sum calculations.
secrets: Write a and d explicitly from the series before solving.
Use T_n = a + (n-1)d and S_n = n/2[2a+(n-1)d].
Keep steps aligned to avoid arithmetic slips.
chapters.mind_map_note: Add a mind map: nth term, sum, AP word-problem patterns.

chapters.name: Triangles
errors: Applying similarity criteria without mentioning the reason.
Mixing up corresponding sides/angles.
Forgetting to conclude the result at the end.
secrets: Write the similarity statement first (e.g., ΔABC ~ ΔPQR).
List corresponding sides in the same order.
End with a clear 'Hence proved'.

questions.text: What is the HCF of smallest prime number and the smallest composite number ?
questions.solution:
Ans. Smallest prime number is 2. Smallest composite number is 4
Therefore HCF is 2.
solution_steps: Ans. Smallest prime number is 2. Smallest composite number is 4
Therefore HCF is 2.

questions.text: Given that 2 is irrational, prove that $(5 + 3\\sqrt{2})$ is an irrational number.
questions.solution:
Ans.
Let us assume that $(5 + 3\\sqrt{2})$ is rational. Then there exist co-prime positive integers a and b such that a 5 3 2 b + = a 3 2 5 b = - a 5b 2 3b - = 2 => is rational. [∵ a, b are integers , ∵ a 5b 3b - is rational]. This contradicts the fact that 2 is irrational. So our assumption is incorrect.
Hence, $(5 + 3\\sqrt{2})$ is an irrational number.
solution_steps: Ans.

Let us assume that $(5 + 3\\sqrt{2})$ is rational. Then there exist co-prime positive integers a and b such that a 5 3 2 b + = a 3 2 5 b = - a 5b 2 3b - = 2 => is rational. [∵ a, b are integers , ∵ a 5b 3b - is rational]. This contradicts the fact that 2 is irrational. So our assumption is incorrect.
Hence, $(5 + 3\\sqrt{2})$ is an irrational number.

questions.text: Find the HCF of 1260 and 7344 using Euclid's algorithm. OR Show that every positive odd integer is of the form 4q + 1 or 4q + 3, where q is some integer.
questions.solution:
Since 7344 > 1260 = 7344×1260 + 5
1044 = +
Since remainder not equal 0 1260 1044 1 216 = + 1044 = 216×4 + 180 = + 216 = 180×1 + 36
180 = 36×5 + 0 = + = +
The remainder has now become zero. ∴ HCF of 1260 and 7344 is 36.
OR
Let a be positive odd integer
Using division algorithm on a and b = 4
a = 4q + r
Since 0 $\le$ r < 4, the possible remainders are 0, 1, 2 and 3 ∴ a can be 4q
OR
4q + 1
OR
4q + 2
OR
4q + 3, where q is the quotient
Since a is odd, a cannot be 4q and 4q + 2 ∴ Any odd integer is of the form 4q + 1
OR
4q + 3, where q is some integer.

questions.text: Prove that $(7 - 2\\sqrt{3})$ is an irrational number, Given that $\\sqrt{3}$ is irrational number.
questions.solution: Assume $(7 - 2\\sqrt{3})$ is rational. Let it be equal to a b where a and b are integers. Then: 7 2 3 a b - = Rearrange: 2 3 7 a b = - Since 3 is irrational, multiplying it by a rational number 2 still results in an irrational number. However, 7 a b - is rational. This contradiction implies that our assumption is wrong. Therefore, $(7 - 2\\sqrt{3})$ must be irrational.

questions.text:
The lines represented by linear equations x = a and y = b (  a not equal b  ) are
(A) intersecting at  (a, b)  .
(B) intersecting at (b, a).
(C) parallel.
(D) coincident.
questions.solution:
The equation x = a represents a vertical line passing through (a, 0).
The equation y = b represents a horizontal line passing through (0, b).
These two lines are perpendicular to each other and intersect at the point (a, b).
Since a ≠ b, they are distinct lines and they are not coincident or parallel.
Therefore, the lines intersect at (a, b).
Correct Option: (A)
solution_steps: The equation x = a represents a vertical line passing through (a, 0).
The equation y = b represents a horizontal line passing through (0, b).
These two lines are perpendicular to each other and intersect at the point (a, b).
Since a ≠ b, they are distinct lines and they are not coincident or parallel.
Therefore, the lines intersect at (a, b).
Correct Option: (A)

questions.text: If$^{-}$5 is a root of the quadratic equation 2x2 + px – 15 = 0 and the quadratic equation p($x^2$ + x)k = 0 has equal roots, find the value of k.
questions.solution: Given –5 is a root of the quadratic equation 2x2 + px – 15 = 0. ∴ –5 satisfies the given equation. ∴ 2(–5)2 + p(–5) – 15 = 0 ∴ 50 – 5p – 15 = 0 ∴ 35 – 5p = 0 ∴ 5p = 35 => p = 7 Substituting p = 7 in p($x^2$ + x) + k = 0, we get 7($x^2$ + x) + k = 0 ∴ 7x2 + 7x + k = 0 The roots of the equation are equal. ∴ Discriminant = $b^2$ – 4ac = 0 Here, a = 7, b = 7, c = k $b^2$ – 4ac = 0 ∴ (7)2 – 4(7)(k) = 0 ∴ 49 – 28k = 0 ∴ 28k = 49 ∴ 49 7 28 4 k = = Marks: 2
solution_steps: Given –5 is a root of the quadratic equation 2x2 + px – 15 = 0. ∴ –5 satisfies the given equation. ∴ 2(–5)2 + p(–5) – 15 = 0 ∴ 50 – 5p – 15 = 0 ∴ 35 – 5p = 0 ∴ 5p = 35 => p = 7 Substituting p = 7 in p($x^2$ + x) + k = 0, we get 7($x^2$ + x) + k = 0 ∴ 7x2 + 7x + k = 0 The roots of the equation are equal. ∴ Discriminant = $b^2$ – 4ac = 0 Here, a = 7, b = 7, c = k $b^2$ – 4ac = 0 ∴ (7)2 – 4(7)(k) = 0 ∴ 49 – 28k = 0 ∴ 28k = 49 ∴ 49 7 28 4 k = = Marks: 2

Therefore first term a = 28

questions.text: Prove that the lengths of tangents drawn from an external point to a circle are equal.
questions.solution: Ans. Given AP and AQ are two tangents from a point A to a circle C (O,r) To prove AP = AQ Construction join OP, OQ and OA Proof In order to prove that AP AQ = , we shall first prove that OPA OQA Δ =Δ since a tangent at any point of a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact. OP AP ∴  and OQ AQ  0 90 OPA OQA =>∠ =∠ = ........(i) Now, in right triangles OPA and OQA, we have 8 OP OQ = [Radii of a circle] OPA OQA ∠ =∠ [from (i)] O A P Q and OA = OA so, by RHS – criterion of congruence, we get OPA OQA Δ Δ ⇒AP =AQ

questions.text: A plane left 30 minutes late than its scheduled time and in order to reach the destination 1500 km away in time, it had to increase its speed by 100 km/h from the usual speed. Find its usual speed.
questions.solution:
Ans.
Let the usual speed of the plane be x km/hr Time taken to cover 1500 km with usual speed 1500 hrs x = Time taken to cover 1500 km with speed of 1500 $\\frac{100}{100}$ x km hr x + = + hrs. 1500 = 1500×1 + 100 2 $x^2$ ∴ = + + 1500 = 1500×1 + 100 2 $x^2$ - = + 100 1 1500 100 2 $x^2$ $x^2$   + - =     +   150000 2 100 $x^2$ = + 10 2 100 300000 0 $x^2$ + - = 2 100 300000 0 $x^2$ + - = 600
OR
500 $x^2$ =- = But speed can’t be negative
Hence usual speed 500 km/hr.

Hence usual speed 500 km/hr.

questions.text: As observed from the top of a 100 m high light house from the sea-level, the angles of depression of two ships are 30$^\circ$ and 45$^\circ$. If one ship is exactly behing the other on the same side of the light house, find the distance between the two ships. [Use 3 1.732 = ]

questions.text:
The first three terms of an AP respectively are 3y – 1, 3y +5 and 5y +1. Then y equals
(A) -3
(B) 4
(C) 5
(D) 2
questions.solution: The first three terms of an AP are 3y-1, 3y+5 and 5y+1, respectively. We need to find the value of y. We know that if a, b and c are in AP, then: $b - a$ = $c - b$ ⇒ 2b = $a + c$ ∴ 2 (3y+5) = 3y – 1 +5y +1 ⇒6y +10 = 8y ⇒10 = 8y -6y ⇒ 2y =10 ⇒ y = 5 Hence the correct option is C.
solution_steps: The first three terms of an AP are 3y-1, 3y+5 and 5y+1, respectively. We need to find the value of y. We know that if a, b and c are in AP, then: $b - a$ = $c - b$ ⇒ 2b = $a + c$ ∴ 2 (3y+5) = 3y – 1 +5y +1 ⇒6y +10 = 8y ⇒10 = 8y -6y ⇒ 2y =10 ⇒ y = 5 Hence the correct option is C.

questions.text:

(A) $\\frac{2}{3}$
(B) $\\frac{1}{6}$
(C) $\\frac{1}{3}$

questions.solution: Total number of possible outcomes = 30 Prime numbers between 1 to 30 are 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, 23 and 29. questions.text: At a point A, 20 metres above the level of water in a lake, the angle of elevation of a cloud is 30$^\circ$. The angle of depression of the reflection of the cloud in the lake, at a A is 60$^\circ$. Find the distance of the cloud from A.
questions.solution: Let AB be the surface of the lake and P be the point of observation such that AP = 20 metres. Let C be the position of the cloud and C’ be its reflection in the lake. Then CB = C’B. Let PM be perpendicular from P on CB. Then m∠CPM=30 and m∠C'PM = 60 Let CM = h. Then CB = h + 20 and C’B = h + 20. In CMP we have, CBSE-X$^{-}$2015 EXAMINATION  $\\frac{20}{23}$ tan30 1 3 3 ...................(i) CM PM h PM PM h = => = => = In ΔPMC' we have, tan60 3 20 20 3

questions.text: In the figure, PQ is a tangent at a point C to a circle with centre O. if AB is a diameter and ∠CAB = 30$^\circ$, find ∠PCA.
questions.solution: In the given figure, In ΔACO, OA = OC …(Radii of the same circle) ∴ ΔACO is an isosceles triangle. ∠CAB = 30$^\circ$ …(Given) ∴∠CAO = ∠ACO = 30$^\circ$ …. (angles opposite to equal sides of an isosceles triangle are equal) ∠PCO = 90$^\circ$ …(radius drawn at the point of contact is perpendicular to the tangent) Now ∠PCA = ∠PCO – ∠CAO ∴ ∠PCA = 90$^\circ$ – 30$^\circ$ = 60$^\circ$ Marks: SET-1  2 /

questions.text: For what value of k will k + 9, 2k – 1 and 2k + 7 are the consecutive terms of an A.P?
questions.solution: If k + 9, 2k – 1 and 2k + 7 are the consecutive terms of A.P., then the common difference will be the same. ∴ (2k – 1) – (k + 9) = (2k + 7) – (2k – 1) ∴ k – 10 = 8 ∴ k = 18 Marks: 1
solution_steps: If k + 9, 2k – 1 and 2k + 7 are the consecutive terms of A.P., then the common difference will be the same. ∴ (2k – 1) – (k + 9) = (2k + 7) – (2k – 1) ∴ k – 10 = 8 ∴ k = 18 Marks: 1

questions.text: The 4th term of an A.P. is zero. Prove that the 25th term of the A.P. is three times its 11th term.
questions.solution: 4th term of an A.P. = a4 = 0 ∴ a + (4 – 1)d = 0 ∴ a + 3d = 0 ∴ a = –3d ….(1) 25th term of an A.P. = a25 = a + (25 – 1)d = –3d + 24d ….[From (1)] = 21d 3 times 11th term of an A.P. = 3a11 = 3[a + (11 – 1)d]  $\\frac{5}{19}$ = 3[a + 10d] = 3[–3d + 10d] = 3 $\times$ 7d = 21d ∴ a25 = 3a11 i.e., the 25th term of the A.P. is three times its 11th term. Marks:

questions.text: In figure, a tent is in the shape of a cylinder surmounted by a conical top of same diameter. If the height and diameter of cylindrical part are 2.1 m and 3 m respectively and the slant height of conical part is 2.8 m, find the cost of canvas needed to make the tent if the canvas is available at the rate of Rs. 500/sq. metre. 22 Use 7 $\pi$   =     7 /
questions.solution: For conical portion, we have r = 1.5 m and l = 2.8 m ∴ S$_{1}$ = Curved surface area of conical portion ∴ S$_{1}$ = $\pi$rl = $\pi$ $\times$ 1.5 $\times$ 2.8 = 4.2$\pi$ $m^2$ For cylindrical portion, we have r = 1.5 m and h = 2.1 m ∴ S$_{2}$ = Curved surface area of cylindrical portion ∴ S$_{2}$ = 2$\pi$rh = 2 $\times$ $\pi$ $\times$ 1.5 $\times$ 2.1 = 6.3$\pi$ $m^2$ Area of canvas used for making the tent = S$_{1}$ + S$_{2}$ = 4.2$\pi$ + 6.3 $\pi$ = 10.5 $\pi$ = 10.5 $\times$ 22 7 = 33 $m^2$  $\\frac{8}{19}$ Total cost of the canvas at the rate of Rs. 500 per $m^2$ = Rs. (500 $\times$ 33) = Rs. 16500. Marks:

(500 $\times$ 33) = Rs. 16500. Marks:

questions.text: What is the common difference of an A.P. in which a21 - a7 = 84 ?
questions.solution: Given a21 – a7 =84 ....................(1) In an A.P a1, $a^2$, $a^3$, a4 ………… an = a1 + (n – 1)d d = common difference a21 = a1 + 20 d ……………..(2) a7 = a1 + 6d ……………….(3) substituting (2) & (3) in (1) a1 + 20d – a1 – 6d = 84 14d = 84 d = 6 ∴ common difference = 6

questions.text: Draw a right triangle ABC is which AB = 6 cm, BC = 8 cm and ∠B = 90$^\circ$. Draw BD perpendicular from B on AC and draw a circle passing through the points B, C and D. Construct tangents from A to this circle. CBSE-X$^{-}$2014 EXAMINATION 13 /
questions.solution: Follow the given steps to construct the figure. Step 1 Draw a line BC of 8 cm length. Step 2 Draw BX perpendicular to BC. Step an arc at the distance of 6 cm on BX. Mark it as A. Step 4 Join A and C. Thus, ΔABC is the required triangle. Step 5 With B as the centre, draw an arc on AC. Step 6 Draw the bisector of this arc and join it with B. Thus, BD is perpendicular to AC. Step 7 Now, draw the perpendicular bisector of BD and CD. Take the point of intersection as O. Step 8 With O as the centre and OB as the radius, draw a circle passing through points B, C and D. Step 9 Join A and O and bisect it Let P be the midpoint of AO. Step 10 Taking P as the centre and PO as its radius, draw a circle which will intersect the circle at point B and G. Join A and G. Here, AB and AG are the required tangents to the circle from A. CBSE-X$^{-}$2014 EXAMINATION  14 /
solution_steps: Follow the given steps to construct the figure. Step 1 Draw a line BC of 8 cm length. Step 2 Draw BX perpendicular to BC. Step an arc at the distance of 6 cm on BX. Mark it as A. Step 4 Join A and C. Thus, ΔABC is the required triangle. Step 5 With B as the centre, draw an arc on AC. Step 6 Draw the bisector of this arc and join it with B. Thus, BD is perpendicular to AC. Step 7 Now, draw the perpendicular bisector of BD and CD. Take the point of intersection as O. Step 8 With O as the centre and OB as the radius, draw a circle passing through points B, C and D. Step 9 Join A and O and bisect it Let P be the midpoint of AO. Step 10 Taking P as the centre and PO as its radius, draw a circle which will intersect the circle at point B and G. Join A and G. Here, AB and AG are the required tangents to the circle from A. CBSE-X$^{-}$2014 EXAMINATION  14 /

questions.text: A cubical block of side 10 cm is surmounted by a hemisphere. What is the largest diameter that the hemisphere can have? Find the cost of painting the total surface area of the solid so formed, at the rate of Rs. 5 per sq. cm. [Use $\pi$ = 3.14]
questions.solution: Side of the cubical block, a = 10 cm Longest diagonal of the cubical block = a 3 = 10 3 cm Since the cube is surmounted by a hemisphere, therefore the side of the cube should be equal to the diameter of the hemisphere. Diameter of the sphere = 10 cm Radius of the sphere, r = 5 cm Total surface area of the solid = Total surface area of the cube – Inner cross‐section area of the hemisphere + Curved surface area of the hemisphere = 6a2 – $\pi$$r^2$ + 2 + $\pi$$r^2$ = 6a2 + $\pi$$r^2$ 2 2 2 6 (10)

questions.text: A man standing on the deck of a ship, which is 10 m above water level, observes the angle of elevation of the top of a hill as 60$^\circ$ and the angle of depression of the base of a hill as 30$^\circ$. Find the distance of the hill from the ship and the height of the hill.
questions.solution: Let CD be the hill and suppose the man is standing on the deck of a ship at point A. The angle of depression of the base C of the hill CD observed from A is 30$^\circ$ and the angle of elevation of the top D of the hill CD observed from A is 60$^\circ$. ∴ ∠EAD = 60$^\circ$ and ∠BCA = 30$^\circ$ In ΔAED, DE tan 60 EA = 3 h x = h = 3 x ....(1) In ΔABC, AB tan 30 BC = 1 10 3 x = x = 10 3 ....(2) Substituting x = 10 3 in equation (1), we get h = 3 $\times$ 10 3 = 10 $\times$ 3 = 30 DE = 30 m  $\\frac{12}{19}$ CD = CE + ED = 10 + 30 = 40 m Thus, the distance of the hill from the ship is 10 3 m and the height of the hill is 40 m. Marks:
solution_steps: Let CD be the hill and suppose the man is standing on the deck of a ship at point A. The angle of depression of the base C of the hill CD observed from A is 30$^\circ$ and the angle of elevation of the top D of the hill CD observed from A is 60$^\circ$. ∴ ∠EAD = 60$^\circ$ and ∠BCA = 30$^\circ$ In ΔAED, DE tan 60 EA = 3 h x = h = 3 x ....(1) In ΔABC, AB tan 30 BC = 1 10 3 x = x = 10 3 ....(2) Substituting x = 10 3 in equation (1), we get h = 3 $\times$ 10 3 = 10 $\times$ 3 = 30 DE = 30 m  $\\frac{12}{19}$ CD = CE + ED = 10 + 30 = 40 m Thus, the distance of the hill from the ship is 10 3 m and the height of the hill is 40 m. Marks:

questions.text: Due to heavy floods in a state, thousands were rendered homeless. 50 schools collectively offered to the state government to provide place and the canvas for 1500 tents to be fixed by the governments and decided to share the whole expenditure equally. The lower part of each tent is cylindrical of base radius 2.8 cm and height 3.5 m, with conical upper part of same base radius but of height 2.1 m. If the canvas used to make the tents costs Rs. 120 per sq. m, find the amount shared by each school to set up the tents. What value is generated by the above problem? 22 Use 7 $\pi$   =    
questions.solution: Height of conical upper part = 3.5 m, and radius = 2.8 m (Slant height of cone)2 = 2.12 + 2.82 = 4.41 + 7.84 Slant height of cone = 12.25 = 3.5 m The canvas used for each tent = curved surface area of cylindrical base + curved surface area of conical upper part = 2$\pi$rh + $\pi$rl = $\pi$r(2h + l) = 22 7 $\times$ 2.8(7 + 3.5) = 22 7 $\times$ 2.8 $\times$ 10.5  $\\frac{13}{19}$ = 92.4 $m^2$ So, the canvas used for one tent is 92.4 $m^2$. Thus, the canvas used for 1500 tents = (92.4 $\times$ 1500) $m^2$. Canvas used to make the tents cost Rs. 120 per sq. m. So, canvas used to make 1500 tents will cost Rs. 92.4 $\times$ 1500 $\times$ 120. The amount shared by each school to set up the tents = 92.4 1500 120 50 = Rs. 332640 The amount shared by each school to set up the tents is Rs. 332640. The value to help others in times of troubles is generated from the problem. Marks:
solution_steps: Height of conical upper part = 3.5 m, and radius = 2.8 m (Slant height of cone)2 = 2.12 + 2.82 = 4.41 + 7.84 Slant height of cone = 12.25 = 3.5 m The canvas used for each tent = curved surface area of cylindrical base + curved surface area of conical upper part = 2$\pi$rh + $\pi$rl = $\pi$r(2h + l) = 22 7 $\times$ 2.8(7 + 3.5) = 22 7 $\times$ 2.8 $\times$ 10.5  $\\frac{13}{19}$ = 92.4 $m^2$ So, the canvas used for one tent is 92.4 $m^2$. Thus, the canvas used for 1500 tents = (92.4 $\times$ 1500) $m^2$. Canvas used to make the tents cost Rs. 120 per sq. m. So, canvas used to make 1500 tents will cost Rs. 92.4 $\times$ 1500 $\times$ 120. The amount shared by each school to set up the tents = 92.4 1500 120 50 = Rs. 332640 The amount shared by each school to set up the tents is Rs. 332640. The value to help others in times of troubles is generated from the problem. Marks:

questions.text:
In Fig. 1, QR is a common tangent to the given circles, touching externally at the point T. The tangent at T meets QR at P. If PT = 3.8 cm, then the length of QR (in cm) is: CBSE-X$^{-}$2014 EXAMINATION $\\frac{2}{29}$
(A) 3.8
(B) 7.6
(C) 5.7
(D) 1.9
questions.solution: It is known that the length of the tangents drawn from an external point to a circle is equal. ∴ QP = PT = 3.8 cm ... (1) PR = PT = 3.8 cm ... (2) From equations (1) and (2), we get: QP = PR = 3.8 cm Now, QR = QP + PR = 3.8 cm + 3.8 cm = 7.6 cm Hence, the correct option is B.
solution_steps: It is known that the length of the tangents drawn from an external point to a circle is equal. ∴ QP = PT = 3.8 cm ... (1) PR = PT = 3.8 cm ... (2) From equations (1) and (2), we get: QP = PR = 3.8 cm Now, QR = QP + PR = 3.8 cm + 3.8 cm = 7.6 cm Hence, the correct option is B.

questions.text:
In Fig. 2, PQ and PR are two tangents to a circle with centre O. If ∠QPR = 46$^\circ$ , then ∠QOR equals: CBSE-X$^{-}$2014 EXAMINATION $\\frac{3}{29}$
(A) 67$^\circ$
(B) 134$^\circ$
(C) 44$^\circ$
(D) 46$^\circ$
questions.solution: Given: ∠QPR = 46$^\circ$ PQ and PR are tangents. Therefore, the radius drawn to these tangents will be perpendicular to the tangents. So, we have OQ ⊥ PQ and OR ⊥ RP. ⇒ ∠OQP = ∠ORP = 90$^\circ$ So, in quadrilateral PQOR, we have ∠OQP +∠QPR + ∠PRO + ∠ROQ = 360$^\circ$ ⇒ 90$^\circ$ + 46$^\circ$ + 90$^\circ$ + ∠ROQ = 360$^\circ$ ⇒ ∠ROQ = 360$^\circ$ - 226$^\circ$ = 134$^\circ$ Hence, the correct option is B.
solution_steps: Given: ∠QPR = 46$^\circ$ PQ and PR are tangents. Therefore, the radius drawn to these tangents will be perpendicular to the tangents. So, we have OQ ⊥ PQ and OR ⊥ RP. ⇒ ∠OQP = ∠ORP = 90$^\circ$ So, in quadrilateral PQOR, we have ∠OQP +∠QPR + ∠PRO + ∠ROQ = 360$^\circ$ ⇒ 90$^\circ$ + 46$^\circ$ + 90$^\circ$ + ∠ROQ = 360$^\circ$ ⇒ ∠ROQ = 360$^\circ$ - 226$^\circ$ = 134$^\circ$ Hence, the correct option is B.

The number of solid of solid spheres, each of diameter 6cm that can be made by melting a solid metal cylinder of height 45 cm and diameter 4 cm is:

(B) 5
(C) 4
(D) 6

Diameter of the sphere = 6 cm ∴ Radius of the sphere, R = 3 cm CBSE-X$^{-}$2014 EXAMINATION  $\\frac{7}{29}$ Let n be the number of solids formed by melting the solid metallic cylinder. ∴ n $\times$ Volume of the solid spheres = Volume of the solid cylinder 3 3 4 180 3 4 180 3 180 3 n 5 4 27 n R n R => $\pi$ = $\pi$ => $\pi$ = $\pi$ => = = k Thus, the number of solid spheres that can be formed is 5. Hence, the correct option is B.

questions.text: Prove that the line segment joining the point of contact of two parallel tangles of a circle passes through its centre.
questions.solution: Let XBY and PCQ be two parallel tangents to a circle with centre O. Construction: Join OB and OC. Now, XB ||AO CBSE-X$^{-}$2014 EXAMINATION  $\\frac{9}{29}$ ⇒ ∠XBO +∠AOB = 180$^\circ$ (sum of adjacent interior angles is 180$^\circ$) Now, ∠XBO = 90$^\circ$ (A tangent to a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact) ⇒90$^\circ$ +∠AOB =180$^\circ$ ⇒∠AOB = 180$^\circ$ - 90$^\circ$ =180$^\circ$ Similarly , ∠AOC = 90⁰ ∠AOB + ∠AOC = 90⁰ + 90⁰= 180$^\circ$ Hence, BOC is a straight line passing through O. Thus, the line segment joining the points of contact of two parallel tangents of a circle passes through its centre.
solution_steps: Let XBY and PCQ be two parallel tangents to a circle with centre O. Construction: Join OB and OC. Now, XB ||AO CBSE-X$^{-}$2014 EXAMINATION  $\\frac{9}{29}$ ⇒ ∠XBO +∠AOB = 180$^\circ$ (sum of adjacent interior angles is 180$^\circ$) Now, ∠XBO = 90$^\circ$ (A tangent to a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact) ⇒90$^\circ$ +∠AOB =180$^\circ$ ⇒∠AOB = 180$^\circ$ - 90$^\circ$ =180$^\circ$ Similarly , ∠AOC = 90⁰ ∠AOB + ∠AOC = 90⁰ + 90⁰= 180$^\circ$ Hence, BOC is a straight line passing through O. Thus, the line segment joining the points of contact of two parallel tangents of a circle passes through its centre.

questions.text: Prove that the tangent drawn at the mid-point of an arc of a circle is parallel to the chord joining the end points of the arc.
questions.solution: In the figure, C is the midpoint of the minor arc PQ, O is the centre of the circle and AB is tangent to the circle through point C. We have to show the tangent drawn at the midpoint of the arc PQ of a circle is parallel to the chord joining the end points of the arc PQ. We will show PQ || AB. It is given that C is the midpoint point of the arc PQ. So, arc PC = arc CQ. =>PC = CQ This shows that ΔPQC is an isosceles triangle. Thus, the perpendicular bisector of the side PQ of ΔPQC passes through vertex C. The perpendicular bisector of a chord passes through the centre of the circle. So the perpendicular bisector of PQ passes through the centre O of the circle. Thus perpendicular bisector of PQ passes through the points O and C. =>PQ  OC AB is the tangent to the circle through the point C on the circle. =>AB  OC The chord PQ and the tangent PQ of the circle are perpendicular to the same line OC. PQ || AB.
solution_steps: In the figure, C is the midpoint of the minor arc PQ, O is the centre of the circle and AB is tangent to the circle through point C. We have to show the tangent drawn at the midpoint of the arc PQ of a circle is parallel to the chord joining the end points of the arc PQ. We will show PQ || AB. It is given that C is the midpoint point of the arc PQ. So, arc PC = arc CQ. =>PC = CQ This shows that ΔPQC is an isosceles triangle. Thus, the perpendicular bisector of the side PQ of ΔPQC passes through vertex C. The perpendicular bisector of a chord passes through the centre of the circle. So the perpendicular bisector of PQ passes through the centre O of the circle. Thus perpendicular bisector of PQ passes through the points O and C. =>PQ  OC AB is the tangent to the circle through the point C on the circle. =>AB  OC The chord PQ and the tangent PQ of the circle are perpendicular to the same line OC. PQ || AB.

questions.text: In figure, from an external point P, two tangents PT and PS are drawn to a circle with centre O and radius r. If OP = 2r, show that ∠ OTS = ∠ OST = 30$^\circ$.
questions.solution: In the given figure, OP = 2r … (Given) ∠OTP = 90$^\circ$ … (radius drawn at the point of contact is perpendicular to the tangent) In ΔOTP, sin ∠OPT = OT OP = 1 2 = sin30$^\circ$ ∠OPT = 30$^\circ$ ∴ ∠TOP = 60$^\circ$ ∴ ΔOTP is a 30$^\circ$-60$^\circ$-90$^\circ$, right triangle. In ΔOTS, OT = OS … (Radii of the same circle) ∴ ΔOTS is an isosceles triangle. ∴ ∠OTS = ∠OST … (Angles opposite to equal sides of an isosceles triangle are equal) In ΔOTQ and ΔOSQ OS = OT … (Radii of the same circle) OQ = OQ ... (side common to both triangles) ∠OTQ = ∠OSQ … (angles opposite to equal sides of an isosceles triangle are equal) ∴ ΔOTQ ≅ ΔOSQ … (By S.A.S) ∴ ∠TOQ = ∠SOQ = 60$^\circ$ … (C.A.C.T)  $\\frac{6}{19}$ ∴ ∠TOS = 120$^\circ$ … (∠TOS = ∠TOQ + ∠SOQ = 60$^\circ$ + 60$^\circ$ = 120$^\circ$) ∴ ∠OTS + ∠OST = 180$^\circ$ – 120$^\circ$ = 60$^\circ$ ∴ ∠OTS = ∠OST = 60$^\circ$ $\div$ 2 = 30$^\circ$ Marks:

In ΔOTS, OT = OS … (Radii of the same circle) ∴ ΔOTS is an isosceles triangle. ∴ ∠OTS = ∠OST … (Angles opposite to equal sides of an isosceles triangle are equal) In ΔOTQ and ΔOSQ OS = OT … (Radii of the same circle) OQ = OQ ... (side common to both triangles) ∠OTQ = ∠OSQ … (angles opposite to equal sides of an isosceles triangle are equal) ∴ ΔOTQ ≅ ΔOSQ … (By S.A.S) ∴ ∠TOQ = ∠SOQ = 60$^\circ$ … (C.A.C.T)  $\\frac{6}{19}$ ∴ ∠TOS = 120$^\circ$ … (∠TOS = ∠TOQ + ∠SOQ = 60$^\circ$ + 60$^\circ$ = 120$^\circ$) ∴ ∠OTS + ∠OST = 180$^\circ$ – 120$^\circ$ = 60$^\circ$ ∴ ∠OTS = ∠OST = 60$^\circ$ $\div$ 2 = 30$^\circ$ Marks:

questions.text: A conical vessel, with base radius 5 cm and height 24 cm, is full of water. This water is emptied into a cylindrical vessel of base radius 10 cm. Find the height to which the water will rise in the cylindrical vessel. 22 Use 7 $\pi$   =    

Now, volume of water in conical vessel = volume of water in cylindrical vessel 1 3 $\pi$r12h1 = r22h2 r12 h1 = 3r22h2 5 $\times$ 5 $\times$ 24 = 3 $\times$ 10 $\times$ 10 $\times$ $h^2$ $h^2$ = 5 5 24 3 10 10 = 2 cm Thus, the water will rise upto the height of 2 cm in the cylindrical vessel. questions.text: A sphere of diameter 12 cm, is dropped in a right circular cylindrical vessel, partly filled with water. If the sphere is completely submerged in water, the water level in the cylindrical vessel rises by 5 3 9 cm. Find the diameter of the cylindrical vessel.
questions.solution: Radius of sphere = r = 6 cm Volume of sphere = 4 3 $\pi$$r^3$ = 4 3 $\pi$ $\times$ (6)3 = 288$\pi$ cm3 Let R be the radius of cylindrical vessel. Rise in the water level of cylindrical vessel = h = 5 3 9 cm = 32 9 cm Increase in volume of cylindrical vessel = $\pi$R2h = $\pi$R$_{2}$ $\times$ 32 9 = 32 9 $\pi$R$_{2}$ Now, volume of water displaced by the sphere is equal to volume of sphere.  $\\frac{11}{19}$ ∴ 32 9 $\pi$R$_{2}$ = 288$\pi$ ∴ R$_{2}$ = 288 9 32 = 81 ∴ R = 9 cm ∴ Diameter of the cylindrical vessel = 2 $\times$ R = 2 $\times$ 9 = 18 cm Marks:

questions.text: Draw a circle of radius 4 cm. Draw two tangents to the circle inclined at an angle of 60$^\circ$ to each other.
questions.solution: Steps of construction: (i) Take a point O on the plane of the paper and draw a circle of radius OA = 4 cm. (ii) Produce OA to B such that OA = AB = 4 cm. (iii) Draw a circle with centre at A and radius AB. (iv) Suppose it cuts the circle drawn in step (i) at P and Q. (v) Join BP and BQ to get the desired tangents.  $\\frac{14}{19}$ Justification: In ∆OAP, OA = OP = 4 cm ... (radii of the same circle) Also, AP = 4 cm ….(Radius of the circle with centre A) ∴ ∆OAP is equilateral. ∴ ∠PAO = 60$^\circ$ ∴ ∠BAP = 120$^\circ$ In ∆BAP, we have BA = AP and ∠ BAP = 120$^\circ$ ∴ ∠ABP = ∠APB = 30$^\circ$ Similarly we can get ∠ABQ = 30$^\circ$ ∴ ∠PBQ = 60$^\circ$ Marks:
solution_steps: Steps of construction: (i) Take a point O on the plane of the paper and draw a circle of radius OA = 4 cm. (ii) Produce OA to B such that OA = AB = 4 cm. (iii) Draw a circle with centre at A and radius AB. (iv) Suppose it cuts the circle drawn in step (i) at P and Q. (v) Join BP and BQ to get the desired tangents.  $\\frac{14}{19}$ Justification: In ∆OAP, OA = OP = 4 cm ... (radii of the same circle) Also, AP = 4 cm ….(Radius of the circle with centre A) ∴ ∆OAP is equilateral. ∴ ∠PAO = 60$^\circ$ ∴ ∠BAP = 120$^\circ$ In ∆BAP, we have BA = AP and ∠ BAP = 120$^\circ$ ∴ ∠ABP = ∠APB = 30$^\circ$ Similarly we can get ∠ABQ = 30$^\circ$ ∴ ∠PBQ = 60$^\circ$ Marks:

questions.text:
If two different dice are rolled together, the probability of getting an even number on both dice, is:

(B) $\\frac{1}{2}$
(C) $\\frac{1}{6}$
(D) $\\frac{1}{4}$
questions.solution: Possible outcomes on rolling the two dice are given below: {(1, 1), (1, 2), (1, 3), (1, 4), (1, 5), (1, 6), (2, 1), (2, 2), (2, 3), (2, 4), (2, 5), (2, 6), CBSE-X$^{-}$2014 EXAMINATION  $\\frac{5}{29}$ (3, 1), (3, 2), (3, 3), (3, 4), (3, 5), (3, 6), (4, 1), (4, 2), (4, 3), (4, 4), (4, 5), (4, 6), (5, 1), (5, 2), (5, 3), (5, 4), (5, 5), (5, 6), (6, 1), (6, 2), (6, 3), (6, 4), (6, 5), (6, 6)} Total number of outcomes = 36 Favourable outcomes are given below: {(2, 2), (2, 4), (2, 6), (4, 2), (4, 4), (4, 6), (6, 2), (6, 4), (6, 6)} Total number of favourable outcomes = 9 ∴ Probability of getting an even number on both dice = Total number of favourable outcomes 9 1 Total number of outcomes 36 4 = = Hence, the correct option is D.
solution_steps: Possible outcomes on rolling the two dice are given below: {(1, 1), (1, 2), (1, 3), (1, 4), (1, 5), (1, 6), (2, 1), (2, 2), (2, 3), (2, 4), (2, 5), (2, 6), CBSE-X$^{-}$2014 EXAMINATION  $\\frac{5}{29}$ (3, 1), (3, 2), (3, 3), (3, 4), (3, 5), (3, 6), (4, 1), (4, 2), (4, 3), (4, 4), (4, 5), (4, 6), (5, 1), (5, 2), (5, 3), (5, 4), (5, 5), (5, 6), (6, 1), (6, 2), (6, 3), (6, 4), (6, 5), (6, 6)} Total number of outcomes = 36 Favourable outcomes are given below: {(2, 2), (2, 4), (2, 6), (4, 2), (4, 4), (4, 6), (6, 2), (6, 4), (6, 6)} Total number of favourable outcomes = 9 ∴ Probability of getting an even number on both dice = Total number of favourable outcomes 9 1 Total number of outcomes 36 4 = = Hence, the correct option is D.

questions.text: The probability of selecting a red ball at random from a jar that contains only red, blue and orange balls is 1 . 4 The probability of selecting a blue ball at random from the same jar 1. 3Ifthe jar contains 10 orange balls, find the total number of balls in the jar.
questions.solution: Here the jar contains red, blue and orange balls. Let the number of red balls be x. Let the number of blue balls be y. Number of orange balls = 10 Total number of balls = $x + y$ + 10 Now, let P be the probability of drawing a ball from the jar P(a red ball) 10 $x^2$ y = + + 1 4 10 4 10 3 10 ............(i) $x^2$ y $x^2$ y x y => = + + => = + + => - = Next, P (a blue ball) 10 y x y = + + 1 3 10 3 10 2 10 ..............(ii) y x y y x y y x => = + + => = + + => - = Multiplying eq. (i) by 2 and adding to eq. (ii), we get 6 2 20 2 10 5 30 6 x y x y $x^2$ - = -+ = = => = Subs. x = 6 in eq. (i), we get y = 8 Total number of balls = $x + y$ + 10 = 6 + 8 + 10 = 24 Hence, total number of balls in the jar is 24. CBSE-X$^{-}$2015 EXAMINATION  12 /
solution_steps: Here the jar contains red, blue and orange balls. Let the number of red balls be x. Let the number of blue balls be y. Number of orange balls = 10 Total number of balls = $x + y$ + 10 Now, let P be the probability of drawing a ball from the jar P(a red ball) 10 $x^2$ y = + + 1 4 10 4 10 3 10 ............(i) $x^2$ y $x^2$ y x y => = + + => = + + => - = Next, P (a blue ball) 10 y x y = + + 1 3 10 3 10 2 10 ..............(ii) y x y y x y y x => = + + => = + + => - = Multiplying eq. (i) by 2 and adding to eq. (ii), we get 6 2 20 2 10 5 30 6 x y x y $x^2$ - = -+ = = => = Subs. x = 6 in eq. (i), we get y = 8 Total number of balls = $x + y$ + 10 = 6 + 8 + 10 = 24 Hence, total number of balls in the jar is 24. CBSE-X$^{-}$2015 EXAMINATION  12 /

questions.text: A card is drawn at random from a well shuffled pack of 52 playing cards. Find the probability of getting neither a red card nor a queen.
questions.solution: There are 26 red cards including 2 red queens. Two more queens along with 26 red cards will be 26 + 2 = 28 ∴ P(getting a red card or a queen) = 28 52 ∴ P(getting neither a red card nor a queen) = 1 – 28 52 = 24 52 = 6 13 Marks:  3 /
solution_steps: There are 26 red cards including 2 red queens. Two more queens along with 26 red cards will be 26 + 2 = 28 ∴ P(getting a red card or a queen) = 28 52 ∴ P(getting neither a red card nor a queen) = 1 – 28 52 = 24 52 = 6 13 Marks:  3 /

questions.text: Two different dice are thrown together. Find the probability that the numbers obtained have (i) even sum, and (ii) even product.
questions.solution: Two dice are through together total possible outcomes 6 6 36 = = (i) Sum of outcomes is even This can be possible when =>Both outcomes are even =>Both outcomes are odd For both outcomes to be Even number of cases = 3 3 = 9 Similarly Both outcomes odd = 9 cases Total favourable cases = 9 + 9 =18 Probability that 18 36 = Sum of the even outcomes is 1 2 (ii) Product of outcomes is even This is possible when =>Both outcomes are even =>first outcome even & the other odd => first outcome odd & the other even Number of cases where both outcomes are even = 9 Number of cases for first outcome odd = 9 and the other Even No. of cases for first outcome odd & the other even = 9 Total favourable cases = 9 + 9 + 9 = 27 Probability = 27 36 3 4 =
